1.exposition: The great chapter opens not with an example but with a definition — and everything that follows is its demonstration. Faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen: it gives present substance to the future and present proof to the invisible, so that what God has promised holds the soul as firmly as what the eye can see. This is no leap in the dark but the settled response to divine testimony. By such faith the elders obtained a good report — the thesis the whole roll will prove: faith is the thing God commends. And its first exercise reaches back before all history: by faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God, the visible called out of the invisible. Faith is thus the proper faculty for the whole God-made order, not merely for religion.
1.original_language: The definition turns on two loaded words. Hypostasis ("assurance") also means "substance," "foundation," or "the underlying reality" — in the papyri it is a title-deed, the document that gives present standing to a future possession — so faith does not merely feel confident about the hoped-for but gives it a real footing in the soul. Elenchos ("conviction") is the proof or demonstration that settles a matter beyond dispute. Together they present faith not as wishful intensity but as an epistemic hold on the unseen as sure as sight is of the seen.
1.historical_context: The definition follows hard upon the Habakkuk citation, "the righteous shall live by faith", and so places the whole chapter within the prophetic tradition of the remnant that trusts God against the visible evidence. The claim that what is seen "was not made out of things that are visible" (v.3) voices the Jewish doctrine of creation out of nothing, implicit in Genesis 1:1 — an origin no one witnessed and no method can verify, received only as divine testimony.
1.christ: The creation-faith of v.3 is quietly christological: the letter has already said the worlds were made "through" the Son (1:2). To believe the universe was framed by the word of God is, in this homily, to trust the same Word who became flesh and entered the sanctuary as high priest. The elders' faith was prospective, straining toward a promise unfulfilled; the readers' is retrospective from the cross — but the object of both is one Lord.

verses.note: The working description of faith: the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen. grammatical-historical Gill insists this is no bare assent to revealed truth nor a faith of miracles, but the special grace of God — "a firm persuasion and expectation, upon the ground of divine testimony." grammatical-historical Robertson reads hypostasis as the title-deed that gives the future a present standing, faith at once making the hoped-for solid and the unseen certain.

Before a single name is spoken, the point they will prove: by it the elders obtained a good report. grammatical-historical Gill takes "the elders" as the ancients — the patriarchs and saints of former ages — testified of and approved by God himself on account of their faith. The whole gallery to come is thus one long proof that faith is what God commends and records.

Faith's first and furthest reach: the worlds were framed by the word of God, so that the visible was not made of what appears. eastern Chrysostom notes that faith needs "a generous and vigorous soul," rising above sense and passing beyond the weakness of human reasonings — for the origin of all things is grasped by faith, not sight. The verse is a witness to creation out of nothing, called into being by God's bare word of command.

witnesses.point: The faith here is not a bare assent nor a faith of miracles but the special grace of God — a firm persuasion resting on divine testimony.
Hypostasis functions like a title-deed: faith gives a present, substantial standing to the things hoped for.
Faith requires a soul that rises above the senses and the weakness of human reasoning; only so can one grasp creation itself.
The definition is no digression but continues the call to patience — faith looks to the unseen and so upholds endurance.

external.name: Harold W. Attridge
external.point: Reads hypostasis in its objective sense — faith is the "reality" or realization of what is hoped for, not merely a subjective feeling of assurance.

1.application: Faith is not the strength of wanting something to be true; it is the reasonable response to a God who has spoken. Order your present conduct around the promises you cannot yet see, and you are already living by faith.

4.exposition: Faith now steps out of definition into history, and the roll begins at the gate of Eden. By faith Abel offered a more excellent sacrifice than Cain, and though murdered he still speaks. By faith Enoch was taken up that he should not see death, having pleased God — and between him and Noah the writer plants the chapter's thesis: without faith it is impossible to please God, for whoever draws near must believe that he is and that he rewards those who seek him. Then by faith Noah, warned of the unseen flood, built the ark, condemning the world and inheriting the righteousness that is by faith. Three men span the antediluvian age: faith in worship, faith in a holy walk, faith in obedient action on a warning no eye could confirm.
4.historical_context: Abel had become, in Second-Temple tradition, the paradigmatic righteous martyr — the first to die for his faith. Enoch's brief translation (Genesis 5:24, "he was not, for God took him") had generated a vast Enoch literature; the writer strips all of it away and grounds his commendation in the single fact that he pleased God. Noah's decades of ark-building on "events not yet seen" (Genesis 6:13–22) are the definition of v.1 made visible in timber.
4.christ: That Abel, though dead, "still speaks" (v.4) anticipates the letter's later contrast: Christ's blood "speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (12:24) — Abel's sacrifice, offered in faith, looked forward to the better sacrifice. Noah became "heir of the righteousness that is by faith" (v.7), the same faith-righteousness the gospel proclaims: the righteousness that pleases God was never a works-righteousness.

verses.note: By faith Abel offered a more excellent sacrifice than Cain, and won God's witness that he was righteous. grammatical-historical Gill locates the excellence not in a greater quantity but in the quality — an offering made in faith, Abel "looking through his sacrifice to the sacrifice of Christ," the lamb a type of the Lamb of God. Though long dead, his faith still preaches across the ages.

By faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death, having the testimony that he pleased God. grammatical-historical Clarke explains that Scripture's "he was not, for God took him" (Genesis 5:24) means he was carried to blessedness without dying; wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds that his testimony came "from God in his own conscience." His walk with God was a walk of faith, crowned by escaping death.

The thesis of the whole catalogue, set between Enoch and Noah: without faith it is impossible to please God. grammatical-historical Gill notes no act of worship — prayer, praise, hearing — is acceptable apart from it. The seeker must believe two things: wesleyan-arminian Wesley marks them plainly — "that he is," and that he rewards those who diligently seek him. In a world of idols, even the first article is a costly confession.

By faith Noah, warned of things not seen as yet, moved with reverent fear, prepared an ark. eastern Chrysostom observes how fitly the writer chooses an image of things unseen — Christ himself compared his coming to "the days of Noah." Building on God's bare word against all appearance, Noah "condemned the world" by the contrast of his obedience and became heir of the righteousness that is by faith.

witnesses.point: Abel's sacrifice excelled in quality because offered in faith; he looked through his lamb to the sacrifice of Christ, the Lamb of God.
Scripture's "God took him" is explained by Hebrews to mean Enoch did not die but was carried to blessedness.
To come to God one must believe both that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him; Enoch's testimony was borne in his own conscience.
Noah, warned of what no eye could see, is a fitting image for a theme of unseen realities, echoing Christ's own word about "the days of Noah."

external.name: F.F. Bruce
external.point: Each example embodies the definition: these are people who took God at his word about realities they could not see and acted accordingly.

4.application: Notice that Noah preached most loudly with a hammer. Faith that acts on an unseen warning — building when the sky is clear — is itself a sermon to a watching world.

8.pericope_label: The Faith of Abraham and the Patriarchs
8.exposition: With Abraham the roll slows and deepens, for here faith is tested to its root. Called, he obeyed and went out, not knowing where he went; he sojourned in the land of promise as a stranger, in tents, because he looked for the city that has foundations, whose builder and maker is God. By faith Sarah received strength to conceive when past age, and from one man, as good as dead, sprang a seed as the stars. Then the writer lifts his eyes: these all died in faith, not having received the promises but greeting them from afar, confessing themselves strangers seeking a better country, a heavenly one. The section climbs to the summit of the Aqedah — Abraham offered up Isaac, reckoning God able to raise the dead — and closes with three deathbeds: Isaac, Jacob, and Joseph, each blessing or charging the unseen future.
8.original_language: Two phrases carry the section's weight. In v.13 the patriarchs confess themselves xenoi kai parepidēmoi — "strangers and exiles," resident aliens with no title to the soil they walk. And in v.19 Abraham "received him back" en parabolē — "in a figure," a parable or type: the son delivered from the very edge of death enacts a resurrection, a living image of the greater raising to come.
8.historical_context: The Aqedah, the binding of Isaac (Genesis 22:1–19), was the most theologically dense narrative in Jewish tradition, and later rabbis debated exactly how the promise could survive Isaac's death — some already crediting Abraham with resurrection-reasoning. To call oneself a stranger and exile echoed a Hellenistic-Jewish idiom for the soul's pilgrimage; the writer bends it toward the future. That "God is not ashamed to be called their God" (v.16) is honor-shame reversal: the divine patron publicly claims his lowly clients — taking the title "the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob" — because they publicly staked everything on his promise.
8.christ: The "city that has foundations" (v.10) reappears as "the heavenly Jerusalem" at the letter's climax (12:22): the patriarchs' pilgrim-faith was faith in the very city Christ's priestly work opens. Abraham's reckoning that "God is able to raise the dead" (v.19) is proto-resurrection faith, fulfilled and proved fact in Christ's own rising — the firstfruits of the life that city holds.

verses.note: By faith Abraham obeyed and went out, not knowing where he went. reformed Calvin names him "the chief father of God's church," the very man in whom the Jews gloried, reminding them that his glory was faith. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the older versions rightly bring in "obeyed" at once — at God's bare call (Genesis 12:1) he left country and kindred for a land not yet shown, walking into pure uncertainty because he leaned on the One who called.

By faith he sojourned in the land of promise as in a strange country, in tents with Isaac and Jacob. grammatical-historical Gill notes that Canaan is called the land of promise because it was pledged by grace, and is itself a type of heaven. The patience of faith held God's promise firm though three generations wandered as homeless strangers in the land, owning not a foot of it.

The secret of that pilgrim-life: he looked for the city which has foundations, whose builder and maker is God. grammatical-historical Gill locates it not in the earthly Jerusalem but the new Jerusalem above. reformed Calvin adds that this reaching beyond the promised land itself "was indeed to see things invisible" — a city with foundations in contrast to his own foundationless tents.

Through faith Sarah received strength to conceive, past age, because she judged him faithful who had promised. grammatical-historical Gill records the debate — whether the faith praised is Sarah's or Abraham's, some copies adding "being barren" to sharpen it. Either way, the heart of faith is here: to rest an impossible promise on the faithfulness of the God who made it, counting that he can and will perform.

From one, and him as good as dead, sprang a multitude as the stars and the sand. grammatical-historical Barnes marks the wonder — the whole Jewish nation descended from a single man, and one whose body was past the power of generation, as the reward of his faith. The countless seed stands as the lasting monument of what trusting God's promise brings to pass.

These all died in faith, not having received the promises, but seeing and greeting them from afar, confessing themselves strangers and pilgrims. eastern Chrysostom makes this "the whole of virtue" — to be a stranger to this world, hanging loose from it as from things not our own. reformed Calvin limits the "all" to the patriarchs, who, having only tasted the promises, despised all the world beside.

They that say such things declare plainly that they seek a country. grammatical-historical Barnes draws the plain inference: no one calls himself a stranger and pilgrim unless he has a home elsewhere toward which he journeys. Their very confession was an open declaration that their hearts were set on a homeland not yet reached.

Had they been mindful of the country they left, they might have returned. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the point: the road back to Chaldea lay open, the journey not impossible, had they wished it. That they never turned back proves their longing was not for the home behind them but for a better country before them.

They desire a better country, a heavenly one; therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared them a city. grammatical-historical Gill takes the better country as heaven itself, the abiding home of God. Far from being ashamed of these homeless pilgrims, God gladly owns them by name and crowns their faith with the everlasting city they sought.

The supreme trial: by faith Abraham, when tested, offered up Isaac. eastern Chrysostom judges this greater than the faith of Abel, Enoch, or Noah, for here it was "faith warring against faith and command against promise." wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the will was accepted as if the deed were done — he offered up the very son through whom every promise ran.

The word that made the command so testing: "In Isaac shall your seed be called". grammatical-historical Barnes explains that the whole promised posterity — the nation and the Messiah himself — was to descend through Isaac and no other. To slay him seemed to slay the promise; God's own command appeared to war against God's own word.

Faith's solution: accounting that God was able to raise him even from the dead, from which he received him back "in a figure." grammatical-historical Robertson sees Abraham holding a command that contravened a promise, and reasoning his way through: the God who gave Isaac in his old age can raise him. grammatical-historical Ellicott reads it as a general truth grasped by faith — "God is able to raise the dead" — and Isaac restored becomes a type of resurrection.

By faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau concerning things to come. eastern Chrysostom notes that the righteous truly foresaw the future by faith, things prophets and righteous men longed to see. Foreseeing nothing by sight, Isaac pronounced by faith the portions God had appointed but not yet shown — the greater blessing falling, by divine overruling, on the younger.

By faith Jacob, dying, blessed both sons of Joseph and worshipped, leaning on his staff. grammatical-historical Gill notes that a dying hour was the patriarchs' time of blessing, so the words might be the more deeply impressed. Too weak to rise, he crossed his hands to set the younger first (Genesis 48:14) and bowed in adoration — faith shining brightest at the deathbed.

By faith Joseph, at his end, spoke of the exodus and gave command concerning his bones (Genesis 50:25). grammatical-historical Gill notes that all Egypt's honors could neither secure him from death nor make him forget it. Dying amid its glory, he so believed God's promise of Canaan that he bound his very bones to that hope, refusing an Egyptian grave.

witnesses.point: Abraham, the father in whom the Jews gloried, was great by faith alone; his hope reached past the promised land to the invisible city, which was to see things unseen.
The city Abraham sought is not the earthly Jerusalem but the heavenly, abiding one; the patriarchs dwelt as strangers in the very land promised them.
At the Aqedah Abraham held a command that contravened a prior promise; his solution was that God, who gave Isaac, could raise him from the dead.
The offering of Isaac surpasses the earlier examples, for here faith warred against faith and command against promise.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: The patriarchs' confession of alien status is not resignation but a deliberate faith-posture: their tent-dwelling is oriented hope toward the city God is preparing.

8.application: Abraham's whole life was lived between a call he could not see the end of and a city he never entered. Faith is content to hold the promise and die still holding it, sure that the God who prepared the city keeps his word past the grave.

23.pericope_label: The Faith of Moses and Others
23.exposition: The scene shifts from the patriarchs' tents to Egypt's court, and faith becomes defiance. By faith Moses' parents hid him, unafraid of the king's edict; grown, he refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter, choosing affliction with God's people over the fleeting pleasures of sin, counting the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt. By faith he left Egypt, unafraid of the king's wrath, enduring as seeing him who is invisible, and kept the Passover and the sprinkling of blood. Then faith passes from the mediator to the whole people and beyond: the sea was crossed, Jericho's walls fell, and Rahab the harlot was spared — an outsider drawn in by faith while a whole city perished in unbelief.
23.historical_context: To refuse to be "called the son of Pharaoh's daughter" (v.24) was to renounce a social identity of staggering privilege — Egyptian royal adoption meant rank, wealth, and a future at the summit of the ancient world's greatest power. Moses' keeping of the Passover (Exodus 12:21–28) and the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 14:22) frame the exodus as faith enacted; the fall of Jericho (Joshua 6:20) opens the conquest. Rahab's inclusion is startling — a Gentile prostitute (Joshua 2:1–21) set beside Abraham and Moses.
23.christ: "The reproach of Christ" (v.26) is the chapter's boldest phrase: Moses bore it centuries before Christ came. As mediator of the old covenant he endured the same category of rejection the mediator of the new would bear — reproach for God's mission, honored by God against human contempt (cf. Romans 15:3). His Passover-keeping (v.28) is likewise christological: blood on the doorposts turned away the Destroyer, pointing to the blood of "our Passover lamb" (1 Corinthians 5:7).

verses.note: By faith Moses was hidden three months by his parents, who feared not the king's commandment. grammatical-historical Gill refers this to the faith of his parents, who, seeing the child "proper" — fair, and marked for God's purpose — defied Pharaoh's decree to drown every Hebrew boy. Fearing God more than the king, they risked all to preserve the child God had appointed.

By faith Moses, come to years, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter. grammatical-historical Gill pictures him full-grown, learned in all Egypt's wisdom, deliberately turning his back on rank, wealth, and the very throne within his reach. By a free and considered choice, faith counted the world's highest glory as loss for the sake of God's despised people.

Choosing rather to suffer affliction with the people of God than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season. grammatical-historical Gill weighs the scales: on one side the delights of Egypt's court, sinful because they meant forsaking God's people, and "for a season" only; on the other, affliction, but with the people among whom God dwells. Faith chose the affliction.

Esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures of Egypt, with respect to the reward. grammatical-historical Ellicott weighs the phrase "the reproach of the Christ," rejecting a merely comparative sense; the shame is bound up with the Messiah himself. wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads it as the reproach borne "for believing in the Messiah to come" — Moses looking off from perishing treasure to the recompense of reward.

By faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the king's wrath, for he endured as seeing him who is invisible. The witnesses divide on the event: grammatical-historical Robertson notes the writer passes over the slaying of the Egyptian and Moses' earlier fear (Exodus 2:14), while reformed Calvin refers it rather to the Exodus, since it stands before the Passover. Either way, the secret is one: Moses held firm by keeping the unseen God ever before his eye.

Through faith he kept the Passover and the sprinkling of blood, that the Destroyer might not touch the firstborn. eastern Chrysostom admires how the writer weaves deep matter into brief words. To slay a lamb and paint a doorpost was a strange rite to mere reason; Moses obeyed and trusted the blood to shield Israel — a blood that types the sheltering blood of Christ.

By faith they passed through the Red Sea as on dry land, which the Egyptians, attempting, were drowned. grammatical-historical Gill pictures the waters standing as walls on either side while Israel walked down into the parted sea. The same path faith trod safely became the grave of unbelief: faith passes where presumption perishes.

By faith the walls of Jericho fell, after they were compassed seven days. grammatical-historical Gill notes the walls fell of themselves, from no natural cause. Israel did nothing but obey a command that to reason seemed foolish — marching silently with priests, ark, and trumpets — and God himself threw down the city. Faith conquers by obeying God's word, however strange the means.

By faith Rahab the harlot perished not with the disobedient, having received the spies in peace. reformed Calvin owns that the meanness of the person might seem to bar her from the roll, yet the Apostle sets a Gentile harlot beside the patriarchs. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes she acknowledged the supremacy of the Lord; her sheltering of the spies at the risk of her life proved a living faith (cf. James 2:25).

witnesses.point: Moses, learned and great in Egypt, freely renounced rank and throne, counting the world's glory loss and choosing affliction with God's people.
The reproach of Christ is the shame Moses bore for believing in the Messiah to come; he looked off from perishing treasure to the coming reward.
Moses' leaving Egypt fits the Exodus itself, and even Rahab, mean as she was, is rightly adduced — faith set the harlot among the fathers.
The writer packs deep matter into few words; the Passover blood, obeyed by faith, sheltered Israel from the Destroyer.

external.name: Peter T. O'Brien
external.point: "The reproach of Christ" reads Moses' suffering typologically: the disgrace he bore for God's people belongs to the same messianic pattern the readers are now called to share.

23.application: Moses made his great renunciation by an act of accounting — weighing Egypt's certain treasures against a reward he could not yet see, and finding the unseen worth more. Every costly obedience runs on the same arithmetic of faith.

32.exposition: Here the writer's breath gives out under the sheer number: "time would fail me to tell" of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets. So he stops naming and starts sweeping — first the triumphs (subdued kingdoms, stopped the mouths of lions, quenched fire, turned armies to flight), then, at a sharp turn, the sufferings (tortured, refusing release for a better resurrection; stoned, sawn asunder, slain; wandering in deserts and caves). Both halves are commended for faith: the criterion is not the outcome but the orientation. And the chapter shuts with a stroke that gathers every name into one hope — all these, commended through faith, did not receive the promise, because God had provided something better for us, that they without us should not be made perfect.
32.original_language: The close is weighted on a single word: they should not be "made perfect" apart from us — teleiōthōsin, brought to their appointed goal or consummation. It is not that the elders lacked faith but that the story lacked its ending; the teleiōsis of Old-Covenant saints waits on the "something better" God kept back — the finished work of Christ that completes the one people of God across both Testaments together.
32.historical_context: The catalogue moves from the judges and David and Samuel into the prophets and, in vv.35–38, into the age of persecution. "Others were tortured, refusing release" evokes the martyrs who chose death over apostasy, expecting a resurrection that made torment bearable. "Sawn asunder" (v.37) was universally understood in early tradition as the martyrdom of Isaiah under Manasseh, and "stoned" recalls Zechariah son of Jehoiada (2 Chronicles 24:21). "Of whom the world was not worthy" (v.38) is apocalyptic reversal: the wanderers the world cast out were its true treasure.
32.christ: "Apart from us they should not be made perfect" (v.40) is the chapter's most concentrated christological claim: the perfecting of the ancients depends on the new covenant's fulfillment in Christ. The whole faith-history was incomplete, straining toward a completion it could not itself achieve. So the "cloud of witnesses" that surrounds the readers (12:1) is not an audience of spectators but a company of those who lived by promise — and the eyes of all are fixed on Jesus, "the pioneer and perfecter of faith" (12:2), in whom both they and we are made complete.

verses.note: "What more shall I say? Time would fail me to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets". grammatical-historical Gill hears the writer confess he has said enough to prove his point yet has not said all, having too much to tell. With a single sweep he gathers Israel's judges, its king, and its prophets into the roll of faith.

Who through faith subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions. grammatical-historical Gill instances David subduing hostile nations and Daniel kept unharmed in the den (Daniel 6:22). Every triumph in the list is ascribed not to human strength but to faith — the secret of all their power.

Quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, out of weakness were made strong, turned to flight the armies of the aliens. grammatical-historical Gill recalls the three Hebrews unhurt in the furnace (Daniel 3:27) and the feeble made mighty, as Samson or Hezekiah. There is no danger faith, leaning on God, cannot overcome.

Women received their dead raised to life; others were tortured, not accepting deliverance, that they might obtain a better resurrection. grammatical-historical Ellicott marks that here the record changes — from the victories of faith in action to the triumph of faith in suffering. wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the martyrs refused release "on sinful terms," scorning rescue that would cost their faith because they looked for a higher rising.

Others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings, of bonds and imprisonment. grammatical-historical Gill points to the prophets so used — mocked, whipped, chained. These bore not only pain but shame: the mockery that wounds the spirit, the scourge that tears the flesh, the prison that robs of liberty. Faith is proved in the patient endurance of contempt no less than in mighty deeds.

They were stoned, sawn asunder, slain with the sword; they wandered in sheepskins and goatskins, destitute, afflicted, tormented. grammatical-historical Clarke and Ellicott identify the stoning with Zechariah (2 Chronicles 24:21) and the sawing asunder with Isaiah, by ancient tradition. eastern Chrysostom confesses that such lives put our ease to shame — we have not tasted even in dreams what those men endured.

(Of whom the world was not worthy) they wandered in deserts and mountains, in dens and caves of the earth. grammatical-historical Gill reads the parenthesis as a vindication: far from being the worthless disturbers the world judged them, these hunted saints were too good for it. The world's verdict is reversed — its refuse was its true treasure.

These all, having obtained a good report through faith, received not the promise. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the word of v.2 resumed: every one won God's testimony, yet none received in life the great promised thing. reformed Calvin argues from the less to the greater — if a small spark of light bore them so bravely to heaven, what should the full sun of the gospel produce in us?

God having provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect. grammatical-historical Gill names the better thing as Christ and the gospel, in which the saints of every age share together. wesleyan-arminian Wesley makes it plain: God's design was "that we might all be perfected together" — the ancients waited for us, that one consummated salvation might crown the whole people of God.

witnesses.point: The "better thing" is Christ and the gospel; the saints of all ages, victors and martyrs alike, are made perfect together in him.
At v.35 the record turns from faith's victories in action to faith's triumph in suffering; God's design was that the elders and we be perfected together.
Argues from the less to the greater: if a spark of light nerved the ancients to such endurance, the full light of the gospel should produce far more in us.
The martyrs refused deliverance on sinful terms for a better resurrection; God kept back the consummation that all the saints might be perfected together.

external.name: Luke Timothy Johnson
external.point: The catalogue works in the ancient tradition of exemplary lists, but its climax subverts the form: the heroes are held incomplete until, with the readers, they are perfected in Christ.

32.application: The roll ends not with the heroes crowned but with the heroes waiting — for us. Their faith and ours belong to one story, and its end is one Christ. Run, then, in the company of a cloud that is cheering you on toward the same finish line.

_meta.title: The Faith of the Elders
reflection: Faith is "the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen" (v.1) — the disposition to order present life around unseen promises. Where are you living by sight where God is calling you to live by faith?
The patriarchs confessed themselves "strangers and exiles," desiring "a better country, a heavenly one" (vv.13–16). What would change if you held your place in this world as a tent, not a foundation?

"Apart from us they should not be made perfect" (v.40): the whole cloud of witnesses waited for the fulfillment now given in Christ. How does belonging to that one, unfinished-then-completed people move you to "run with endurance" (12:1)?
